TRUSTPB represents a preferred diagnostic approach for rectal and perirectal lesions inaccessible or nondiagnostic by standard colonoscopy, including malignant tumors, gastrointestinal stromal tumors (confirmed by CD117 or DOG1 positivity), high-grade intraepithelial neoplasia, and benign lesions including rare cases of endometriosis. This technique addresses critical diagnostic needs in rectal cancer evaluation while providing a safe, accurate alternative when conventional endoscopic methods prove inadequate.

title: Translational Application of Biomedical Microrobots in Female Reproductive System
summary:
# Summary

Biomedical microrobots represent a promising translational technology for multiple female reproductive health applications, including infertility treatment, gynecological cancer therapy, and bacterial infection management. These microscale devices function as intelligent delivery vehicles capable of transporting drugs, cells, and genes through the complex reproductive environment. A particularly innovative approach involves functionalizing spermatozoa with magnetic nanoparticles to create "spermbots" that leverage sperm's natural ability to navigate viscous, acidic media; these human spermbots have demonstrated efficacy in treating cervical and ovarian cancer through combinatorial drug delivery with agents like doxorubicin and camptothecin. Microrobot fabrication and performance are enhanced through various strategies including scaffold materials (alginate, gelatin, collagen, silk), magnetic or ultrasound-based actuation systems, and biofunctionalization with extracellular matrix proteins like fibronectin and hyaluronic acid. By addressing multiple bottlenecks in assisted reproductive technologies—including poor oocyte activation, implantation failure, and compromised endometrial conditions—microrobots offer innovative solutions to overcome current limitations in fertility treatments. The integration of stimuli-responsive materials and enhanced motility mechanisms positions microrobotics as a transformative platform technology for personalized gynecological medicine.

title: Evaluating nursing student confidence after sexual health training workshop
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated the effectiveness of a sexual health training workshop for nursing students, addressing significant barriers that prevent healthcare professionals from conducting comprehensive sexual health assessments. Prior to training, most third-year nursing students lacked knowledge and confidence in sexual health discussions due to embarrassment, stigma, insufficient clinical opportunities, and multiple contextual barriers including privacy concerns, time constraints, and lack of supervisor support. The educational intervention incorporated the 6Ps Framework for sexual and reproductive health assessment, trauma-informed care principles, and case-based learning on conditions including HIV and sexually transmitted infections. Following the workshop, students demonstrated statistically significant improvements across all measured domains, including knowledge, confidence, and comfort in discussing sexual health as a fundamental component of holistic care. The findings demonstrate that targeted educational interventions can effectively overcome psychological and knowledge-based barriers, equipping nursing students with the competencies necessary to integrate sexual health assessment into patient-centered practice.

title: Cryopreservation of ovarian tissue – what's known so far and future perspectives
summary: Ovarian tissue cryopreservation (OTC) has emerged as a critical fertility-preservation strategy for women and prepubertal girls facing gonadotoxic cancer treatments, as well as patients with non-malignant conditions including endometriosis and autoimmune diseases. The primary challenge in successful OTC involves minimizing follicle loss during the cryopreservation process, with the ischemic revascularization period causing approximately 68% of primordial follicle loss compared to only 7% during the freezing process itself. Cryoprotective agents such as DMSO and propanediol-sucrose preserve primordial follicles by reducing ice crystal formation and binding intracellular water; DMSO specifically creates water pores that facilitate replacement of intracellular water with cryoprotectants. Slow freezing protocols currently employ cooling rates of 0.3°C/min using DMSO at concentrations around 1.5M or 10%, though this process still induces lipid peroxidation that leads to oxidative injuries, membrane damage, DNA fragmentation, and programmed cell death. Clinically, reimplanted cryopreserved ovarian tissues have resulted in over 200 healthy live births and can restore both gametogenic and steroidogenic hormonal functions. Future optimization efforts should focus on minimizing ischemic injury during revascularization and further improving cryoprotectant formulations to enhance follicle survival rates.

title: Evaluating Sequencing Strategies for Endometrial Microbiome Profiling in Endometrial Cancer: A Comparative Study of Short‐ and Long‐Read 16S rRNA Approaches
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluates sequencing methodologies for characterizing the endometrial microbiome in endometrial cancer, addressing the challenge that microbial dysbiosis is associated with endometrial cancer development. Microbial imbalance in the endometrium appears to promote carcinogenesis through multiple pathways including local inflammation and immune modulation. The researchers compared long-read Oxford Nanopore Technologies (ONT) sequencing with short-read Illumina MiSeq sequencing, finding that ONT sequencing substantially improved taxonomic resolution and detection of low-abundance bacterial taxa while achieving higher read counts (mean 677,000 versus 110,000 reads) and success rates (87% versus 75%). However, both platforms struggled with contamination in low-biomass samples, though the study identified that freezing samples prior to DNA extraction improved bacterial detection and community consistency. Overall, the findings support ONT as a superior approach for endometrial microbiome profiling and highlight the uterine microbiome as an important factor in endometrial cancer pathogenesis, warranting further investigation into microbial contributions to disease development.

title: Positioning double-balloon enteroscopy in the diagnostic algorithm for suspected small bowel Crohn’s disease: a retrospective analysis of decision-making at a tertiary referral centre
summary:
# Summary

Small bowel capsule endoscopy (SBCE) serves as the primary initial investigation for suspected small bowel Crohn's disease, demonstrating high sensitivity (89.6%) and specificity (86.2%) with superior diagnostic accuracy compared to magnetic resonance enterography (67.9%) and computed tomography enterography (52.3%). However, SBCE's major limitation is its inability to obtain tissue for histological confirmation or perform therapeutic interventions, a gap addressed by double-balloon enteroscopy (DBE), which enables both biopsy sampling and therapeutic procedures such as stricture dilation. Clinical factors predicting escalation to DBE include diagnostic uncertainty on SBCE, increasing patient age, and presenting symptoms of persistent diarrhea or blood loss, with DBE impacting management in 77-82% of patients with suspected or confirmed Crohn's disease. The analysis reveals that SBCE alone established or excluded inflammatory bowel disease in 70% of patients, while DBE confirmed diagnosis in 40% of suspected cases and 63% of previously diagnosed cases from outside institutions. Key imaging features favoring Crohn's disease include cobblestone appearance and longitudinal ulcers, and several conditions including small bowel lymphoma, NSAID-induced enteropathy, infectious enteritis, tuberculosis, ischaemia, vasculitis, Behçet's disease, endometriosis, and drug reactions can mimic Crohn's disease, emphasizing the importance of histological confirmation through DBE when diagnostic uncertainty exists.
